speaker: interviewer
text: --is Linda Burr. I'm interviewing Reuben Drehspul in Johannesburg, South Africa, October 16, 1995. Tape five. Reuben, can you continue telling us about Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Hungarian Jewish arrived--

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]. I'm ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you continue telling us what happened at Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Definitely. When I'd been there for a month or two, a transport of Hungarian Jews arrived. They came direct from Budapest to our camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They came well-fed, well-groomed, and well-looking. Complete families, father, mother, and children. The children were separated. The women were sent to the women's camp. In our-- in camp, we had a women's camp as well, and the men camp. They suffered straight bad luck, the Hungarian Jews, in our camp. Because it was a shock to them. They-- from goodness to-- to nothing. To degradation. It was a big shock for them. For us, it took five years to come to the camp. For four years, we took it gradually. But for them, it was the biggest surprise. They were stripped of their dignity, and they endured really big hardship. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Their hair was shaved, and the same-- they had the same treatment as we had. And they went to go to different working places. I still worked as a messenger boy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And things became bad, with the food, with the-- the lice. We got lice big as cockroaches. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Take them and just pull them off. Or you take off your shirt, and you put it next to the little stove in the barrack. It's like a machine gun, tut tut tut. You're killing them off. Especially under the arms. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and the Germans saw that we got lice. And this, they built it-- they built in-- outside our camp and [SPEAKING GERMAN] had a disinfectant department to shower every two weeks. Before this, we used to walk 10 kilometers to another camp to have a bath where they used to spray or something to do with our clothing. But it didn't help much. But then when they built in our camp, outside our camp, two hundred meters, they used-- we used to have a shower, a hot shower. And they used to steam our clothes that we had. The blue and white striped uniform. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this helped us a lot. Otherwise, we wouldn't survive a day. The-- the-- the-- the lice, they suck out your blood, and you die or you get diseased. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] while I was working in the camp, I had already a feeling it's, for me, not good to be in the camp. It's not right. We are only four children. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One, two, three, four. Four children. As I say, I'm-- we were two from Lithuania, one from Hungary, and another one from somewhere, I'm not sure. Anyway. So I-- so I spoke to my father, I should give up the job in the camp and go work outside the camp at [NON-ENGLISH] or maybe somewhere else. So he said, the best thing, maybe, would be for you-- you'll work with me, as my helper. He'll speak to his German supervisor. And he spoke to the German supervisor. He said, all right, if he can manage to go into this-- to this marching out with-- with this particular unit, he's all right. He can work here. But what happened, he didn't tell him that I was only 14 years old. He thought, he thought electrician, 20. But 14 years old, he didn't tell him. And I couldn't give up my job so easily. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It took a few months, but in the meanwhile, I got very sick. I got double pneumonia, or ammonia, whatever it is. Seeing I knew the doctors. And I knew a-- a certain German in our camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A big SS man. He used to beat the people to death. He used to kick them everywhere. For me, he had respect. Or he felt sorry for me. He used to give me bread. He used to give me a piece of cheese, when I was still running around. Here, he was informed that Robert-- they called me Robert-- he is sick. He came to visit me in my barrack. He said, you junge-- you boy. I don't want you to report to the hospital. To the [INAUDIBLE]. The doctor will come to you. And I'll see to the food. And he used to beat everybody to death. Such a monster. For me, he felt sorry. I didn't ask him if he's got a child my age, or he's got a brother my age, or whatever it is. He had a few big [INAUDIBLE]. A big noise. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he arranged the doctors to come to visit me. And they found out, without x-ray, that I'd got pleurisy. They put in the stethoscopes straight away. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he brought papers from the German hospital, from the camp, but for the German soldiers to-- to write a prescription for what I need.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the Jewish doctor, Dr. Elkas, wrote out-- [CHUCKLES] I need-- I need injections and some medicine. You think he didn't go to a German hospital in Landsberg, and got for me. I got injections, and I got everything against pneumonia. And he said to me, one day, why are you losing weight, Reuben, or Robert, that's what they called me in those days. Or Laufer. There was another guy, who used to call me Arschloch. Excuse me. You know what is Arschloch? Backside. [CHUCKLES] If they curse a Jew man or a Jew women, you used to call them arschloch. Excuse me, bitte. [SPEAKING GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: You say, well, I haven't got enough food. The doctors told me you don't eat at all. I say, I can't eat this, I can't eat this. He said, right. Every two days, he used to bring me sandwiches, with bacon, with ham, with meat, with cheese, with this. And he brought me milk and cream sometimes. And I was sick for three weeks. I got better. But I was very weak. And this Levy, the Hungarian Jew-- I became, somehow, friendly with him. His coworker didn't like me, although his son was working with me. But this scriber, this writer, the book he put out, whatever, he kept this camp on their toes. He knew everybody. Every number. Everything he knew. And he used to-- not correspond, but he used to-- he used to speak between the Germans and himself. When they used to come to his office, he used to show them everything. So-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and I got better. And the doctors used to come to visit me in my barrack. They used to send in cleaner boys. Cleaner-- inmates to make my bed. To shake up the blanket, and [INAUDIBLE] straw, whatever [INAUDIBLE]. And to spray some-- DDT, or not-- they didn't have DDT, but different killing material. Some gas. Maybe gas what they gassed the people, they gassed the cockroaches too. I survived. But I was weak. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I-- I went back to work [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as a messenger boy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then I decided [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that it's a good idea to-- to-- to go away from this camp. Seeing new people coming in, and the old ones, they'll liquidate. It's not a proposition. They don't get-- they don't let people to get-- to get acclimatized. If you stay in one place too long, they know you're getting too clever. So anyway, took me some doings to work with my old man, let him rest in peace. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I was still running as a Laufer, working in the camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And one day came the order in the morning that the camp must be cleaned. Spotlessly cleaned because there's going to be an-- an inspection from a general from Zurich, Switzerland, to come to see the camp. And a delegation from the Red Cross. And a German High Command is coming to check the camp. 1944-45 was this already. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we cleaned the camp this day and the next day and the day after. The people went to work, and we worked in the camp. 11 o'clock, the whistle goes. Everybody must go to the appellplatz, where they counted people. Where they speak to the people. They counted people.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the all inmates who worked in the camp had to assemble in this place. They didn't come from Geneva. And they didn't come from Switzerland. And they didn't come from nowhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: Came High Command Gestapo. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and they picked out the people that are already half-dead, Muselmann, means people that are half-dead or dead already, to go to different sites or to take their number.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this German officer comes up to me. He says, you! You, boy, what are you doing here? You should have been a long time ago in the chimney. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he walks around me. How old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Obviously, I told him-- I told him, Obersturmfuhrer, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Obersturmfuhrer is from the Chief SS. I'm 18 years old. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And he tells the other guy, other German, [SPEAKING GERMAN], and he took my number. 8482, whatever I'd got there. And now, once they take your number, it's a matter of hours or days. And they went onto the next row. With my little luck-- I can laugh now. It was no laughing matter at all. I passed by this Hungarian schreiber. I said, Mr. Levy, he took my number!

speaker: interviewee
text: This man became white as snow. He said what man? I [INAUDIBLE]. And he had the courage to go up to this German, which he never saw before, and he went up to him, and he-- and he spoke to him in German about me. Who am I? A little guy. He said, Obersturmfuhrer, you took the number [SPEAKING GERMAN], from the [SPEAKING GERMAN] Messerschmidt [NON-ENGLISH]. So he looked at him. Who are you to tell me what I did or not? But this German took notice. He came back to me with this-- schreiber, with this clerk, whatever you call it. Ad-- administrator. And he came up to me and looked me up. Like this, like this. Looked me in the eyes. [SPEAKING GERMAN] How old are you? [SPEAKING GERMAN] Obersturmfuhrer. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Ach, so! Ah, so. You still got luck. And he scratched off the name on his board. This helped me out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I spoke to my father. I had enough. And I spoke to Levy. So, he said to me, Reuben, you had enough. You go to work with your father. We'll make sure you go into this-- to this [INAUDIBLE]. To this-- to this-- the people work together [INAUDIBLE]. You'll work with your father in the-- in the same company. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So the next morning, four o'clock, we get a piece of bread, a piece of bologna, black coffee. And we're marching for half an hour. [INAUDIBLE] the workplace. I come to after 6, 7 o'clock. And my father said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Get yourself warmed up a little bit. We'll make ourselves something to drink in the workshop. You'll be all right, Reuben.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all of a sudden, comes in a German, and he shouts, whose dog is this? Pointing at me. Whose dog is this? So my father went up and said, this is my son. So he said, [SPEAKING GERMAN] I thought you got a big son. This is a dog, what you brought me. What can he do here? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So my father said, all right. You don't want to speak to me about him today. We'll put him to work somewhere else. And I'll speak to you maybe later or tomorrow.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- so I went out from the workshop, and my father said, you go straight and a little bit left, they're chopping the trees to make more place for some other stuff. And I just off and sleep the whole day-- unless they catch you, the Germans, and then they finish your life anyway.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went in between the trees where they cut down-- the leaves. You know, they got these pine trees. Big ones. And I slept all day, so nice in the sun. [CHUCKLES] And when they marched by, the people-- I-- I jumped in between the people, and I marched back to the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the camp, they gave me this plate of water soup and a piece of bread. Now I'll tell you what was this special bread, so good. The Germans didn't like the bread. There must have been something wrong with the bread. It was wrong. It was moldy. But they didn't know it's penicillin. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They didn't know it's penicillin in it. And this kept us going. This was antibiotic. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't get so much sores. Although when we used to cough, it used to come as green smoke. One guy said, why do you always cough green smoke? Excuse me. There is going to come a day you'll smoke a cigarette.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I got liberated, the Yankee army-- American army, bless them-- gave me cigarettes. Gave me a lot of things. And this was the story about the bread. The rejects. It was moldy, they gave it to us. And this helped us out. Now this German who brought me the parcels while I was sick, and one day came with a big box. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A heavy box. It was bacon, it was chicken-- cooked chicken. Every-- smoked or cooked. And bologna and [INAUDIBLE], and some other stuff. And he came up to me. I was still in bed then. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody-- I didn't have to go out in the morning to be counted. He fixed up the lot. And he said to me, Robert, or Reuben-- Robert-- Robert, [SPEAKING GERMAN] I'm very sorry. I brought you a present. I said, present, why? He said, I'm going away to the Russian front. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: He shook hands with me, and he went away. And he told them, not-- none of the inmates should pinch my food. Because if they pinch his food, the-- the ones that pinched my food-- and they wanted to shoot the guy. I begged the schreiber, don't tell them. Otherwise they'll shoot him. I would not be able to eat. And they notified the barrack. Anybody who pinched my food-- [INAUDIBLE]. Death penalty. Nobody dared to touch my food, and I survived. And when I got better, I walked in with my late father in there in that moat. And when he chased me out, the first day-- he doesn't want a dog here-- my father persuaded him that I should be his helper. And he told him that for the last three years I worked in the ghetto as a helper, electrician, and a sewing machine mechanic. So he agreed to it. I shouldn't be in his way, he doesn't mind. But-- but he should have told him I'm only 15 years old. He said, you didn't ask his age. Anyway, I came there the next day. I saw him. So he said, name this worksite, take the tools, and [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And go out of here. So, all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I used to work with my father. Test this motor, and test this [INAUDIBLE], and check this. And stop to the Italian kitchen there. They had a kitchen, we used to eat. Well, we had the Greeks. We used to get organized.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't want-- I said-- my father said, Reuben, sell the [INAUDIBLE] to the Greeks or the Italians to get some better food. For me, it's enough to eat. I don't have to starve myself, and I can't take it to the camp. Because you come to the camp, they search you in case you've got a potato. I got liberated with the four coins and the watch and the Russian rubles. But what's just to eat. I've forgotten to tell you. While I was a Laufer, a messenger boy in the camp, and when my late father was still alive, my late father came in one day from camp and organized a [INAUDIBLE]. It's a canister of German army soup from the other kitchen. And I said to him, Pa, [SPEAKER GERMAN] Dad, you don't have to stay in the queue to get water soup. I got a container for your soup. But please, give my Uncle Isaac half of it, at least. Yes, Reuben, yes, yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Two hours later, they're looking for me, the-- the Germans. Reuben, your father is dying. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He's swole up. He's dying. What happened? He overate himself. He finished himself the two little soups. That's how people are. You know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I myself used to stay, when I was working there, sometimes. No, before-- when I was in the camp as the Laufer, the German used to peel off the crust from his bread, because he couldn't eat the hard piece. I used to pick it up, and I used to eat it afterwards. But there was a good place. We used to get food. Not too bad. But the Germans didn't supply much. We used to pinch. Or we used to get from the Italian to trade in this or make another swap. Then we used to get Russian tobacco, [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What can you do with tobacco if they haven't got a pipe and they haven't got paper? What can you do with this? You can't even plant it. It doesn't grow. I find a plan. I used to pinch from the office, typing paper. Not the thick ones, the copy ones. The thin ones. And I used to sell it for food in the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: A guy used to give away his last piece of bread to-- to-- to make a-- to make a-- a cigarette. And that's how it was. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And although it was hard for me, and every day, the-- the Allied planes used to fly over-- there used to be alarms, sirens, [NON-ENGLISH]. But they bombed us once, on the factory, on top. Some bombs exploded. Some bombs just slide down. When the bombs fall down, and it doesn't-- you know, the center pin of the bomb doesn't explode. It just slided down. And when the planes went away, used to come the Germans. They used to dismantle the detonator. They used to take out whatever it is. And they used to send it to the-- to the German airfields to bomb the-- the other side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Reuben, tell me about the last days in the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: The last day was very sad. We were supposed to go to work. We come back from work, and they said it's quarantined. No work anymore. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What happened? [INAUDIBLE] quarantine. The next day, we are assembled on the big platz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they didn't count us. And a big speaker, a German officer, he said, tomorrow morning, you're going to be exchanged in Switzerland against Germany's prisoners of war. You'll be free people. People who can't walk, transport will be provided. Food-- some will be provided. Otherwise, you'll get it in Switzerland, which is not far. 400 kilometers. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Everybody said, [INAUDIBLE], Reuben! We're just going to go by train! No, no, no, no, no. I don't want to be locked in nowhere. I want to walk. Better to walk. And we marched one night, and we arrived in Dachau's main camp. There are seven crematoriums out there, burning. And we arrived in this main camp, Dachau. There is coaches. Budapest, Budapest, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Munchen, or-- or something else, or Dachau, whatever it is. When the-- the coaches came in to Dachau, main camp, they didn't manage to put them through the crematoriums. They gassed them in the trains. The-- the-- the scaffolding, the heating pipe, or the water pipe, or whatever was arranged. And when we arrived, they were staying at the windows-- excuse me-- with their tongues out. Gassed to death. Some were lying, some were standing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we'd been there all night in this camp, in the main camp. [INAUDIBLE]. People came from all other camps. I was number one. There was number two. There were number seven, number three, number this, came to this camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: They bombed the city, the Americans. Dachau. But our main camp was lit up. Not a single bomb fell there. Us bomb-- the Americans understood. Why should the Germans light up a place? Or it's a camouflage, or it's enemies, or it's something. They didn't bomb us. Next morning, we were told to report to the main platz. And everybody was given a closed tin of food. How can you open a tin with-- with your teeth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, we marched out with a tin of some food. And we walked, and we walked for days and nights. And we joined up with other Jewish people from other camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: We managed to open the tin. We'd find [INAUDIBLE], we'll find this. We opened. Guess what it was? What it was. It was better than gold. Gold you can't eat. It was pig's fat. Pure white fat.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some people died from it. They took it in one go. And they couldn't run fast enough. They died from diarrhea. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A lot of us died after the war, of overeating themselves. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we marched for a long time. Three days. We passed a few bridges. And I could see dynamite cases with electric wires [INAUDIBLE] and the Germans staying there just to get an order to blow the bridges. One section of another bridge would pass by, and they'd blow up the people and the bridge. Some people remained on this side of the bridge, and some people had to march forward another day or two. As I passed a bridge with my late father and uncle, the bridge blew up. People died on the bridge. And those people who remained on the other side got liberated the same day. These were the lucky ones. The unlucky were in the middle. Went down with the bridge. We still suffered another day. And we marched.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the mountains, mountains, and snowing, and raining, and cold. Through the whole mountains. And we were lying in a forest. And everybody said, you see right in the bottom? There they're going to shoot us. And the snow will cover us up. And nobody will find us. Not even a dog will be able to smell there were people there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, we came to this forest. And we slept over in-- in-- in the night. And it was cold. And hunger. And wet. And in the morning, someone gives me a [SPEAKING GERMAN]. It means a-- a shake.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING GERMAN] to hear, I'm still alive. The Americans are here. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We didn't see, but it was Americans. A few came in, secretly. Not completely troops. How do you say, people who-- who find us. How do you call it? Whatever it is, they came first, before the troops are moving in. And they say, it's a matter of an hour or two. The American army will be here. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: The American army came in. They liberated us. Came tanks. They opened the hatches. They dropped boxes the size of this. A breakfast box. Five biscuits. Five cigarettes. Five pieces of chewing gum. Chocolate. A tin of cheese. Milk powder and some other stuff. And then came the-- the infantry. And they gave us plenty of food and cigarettes. And then came an order that you have to go into the city. It was not far. The city [INAUDIBLE]. Some people got allocations in hotels. A few hotels were there. And some people had to stay with Germans as guests until the American army cleared out some German [INAUDIBLE], where they keep troops. And we had to march. I had to march to a certain military hospital, for the SS only. A [INAUDIBLE] hospital. I stopped a jeep, and it gave me a lift there. And this hospital, the German hospital, was very big. And they employed German prisoners to clean the hospital. But they had a hell of a job to clean it. The Americans fed us very little. But we thought we were clever. But there were cleverer people than me. I stayed three times in the queue to get three meals. They didn't give us any milk. Then may have given us rice, porridge, not to get a runny stomach, and not loose food. No fried eggs, nothing. And a lot of biscuits. I actually [INAUDIBLE] a tin of biscuits which lasted for a month, I was eating it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
